note: Derived viral teardowns of real breakout videos (over-reached the creator's following). Our analysis in our words; no verbatim transcript or source URL. The full audit version stays local.

teardowns.openingVisual: A dark-skinned creator in a grey headwrap and pink zip-top, head tilted up, holding a makeup brush loaded with near-black product to her cheek; a small inset box in the lower-left shows the original lighter-skinned creator she is responding to.
teardowns.lighting: Soft, even key light against a clean light-grey/cream room; flattering and bright, not low-fi.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Tight selfie close-up, face fills the frame, with a stitch/inset window pinning the original creator she is clapping back at.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Minimal, deliberately understated — headwrap, gold hoops, dewy skin — so all attention is on the brush and the deadpan delivery.
teardowns.hookTechnique: She literally paints the 'darkest shade' foundation on as 'black face paint,' turning a shade-range complaint into a visual that you cannot scroll past because you need to know whether she is serious.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The duet structure plants an unresolved conflict — you keep watching to see how far she takes the bit and how she lands the comeback at the original creator.
teardowns.viralMechanism: It rides an active discourse (foundation shade inclusivity, @golloria) and weaponizes deadpan satire; viewers share to take a side, and the caption 'you all way too dramatic' is itself a quote-able stance that drives the comment war.

teardowns.openingVisual: A woman in glasses filling the whole frame, face caked in still-unblended powder and bright green eyeshadow with a flushed under-eye, looking dead into the lens; large yellow caption 'makeup touch up' sits over her chin.

teardowns.cameraFraming: Extreme handheld selfie close-up, slightly low angle, so the in-progress face is unavoidable.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Anti-glam: visible flyaways, mid-process makeup, ordinary clothes — the gap between 'tutorial' framing and the chaotic result is the entertainment.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Frames itself as a normal 'touch up' tutorial while showing a face that looks anything but finished, so the viewer stays to see whether it's serious, satire, or a trainwreck.
teardowns.retentionDevice: An ongoing 'is she actually going to fix this?' open loop, plus the 'Dad, is that you?' interruption mid-video that resets attention.
teardowns.viralMechanism: It works as forced-productivity comedy: the value is watching someone perform a beauty ritual with zero polish and full confidence, which reads as funny-relatable and gets stitched/dueted; the absurd length (288s) survives because each segment adds a new bit.

teardowns.openingVisual: A tiny fluffy brown bunny standing upright on its hind legs beside a yellow duckling, both positioned at the base of a small branch with oversized digitally-composited strawberries hanging from it, set in an outdoor leafy garden at golden hour
teardowns.structureArc: The video opens and holds a single enchanting scene: a baby bunny standing on its hind legs sniffing and nibbling at a digitally-enhanced strawberry on a branch, with a yellow duckling waddling alongside it. Across all frames (0.3s through 9.1s) the camera slowly creeps closer, tightening the frame from a wider medium shot to a more intimate close-up of the bunny actively eating the strawberry. There is no plot turn or reveal — the payoff is cumulative cuteness as the bunny progressively engages more actively with the fruit while the duckling intermittently noodles around in the background. The ending frame shows the bunny biting directly into the strawberry face-on, fully committed, maximum adorability.

Mid-video the prongs grip the egg from underneath and the egg lifts cleanly off the pan surface. The flip is executed and the egg is set back down now over-easy with the yolk intact. teardowns.viralMechanism: Pure 'I need this' impulse purchase energy; frictionless share-to-a-friend format ('tagging my partner' / 'my husband needs to see this'); the simplicity of the problem-solution triggers 'why doesn't everyone have this' reactions in comments

teardowns.openingVisual: Overhead shot of a cluttered black granite sink full of dirty dishes and food debris, hand reaching in

teardowns.viralMechanism: Aspirational domestic satisfaction — the spotless black sink is an aesthetic flex. Cleaning ASMR triggers saves for 'how to clean my sink.' teardowns.retentionDevice: The single-take real-time push-up set with no cuts creates implicit counting behavior in viewers — people watch to count reps and can't stop mid-count. teardowns.retentionDevice: Each cut delivers a new conceptual fashion moment — the runway compilation format creates an implicit 'what's next?' loop. teardowns.openingVisual: Woman in medical scrubs with stethoscope seated at wooden table, empty clear cup in front of her, speaking directly to camera
teardowns.structureArc: Opens with a credentialed-looking nurse/clinician at a desk starting a demonstration with an empty cup. Within 2 seconds an animated anatomical diagram (breast sagging/estrogen) appears overlaid, establishing a health problem. She adds green powder then pours water, narrating a chemistry demonstration — cup turns green then red (labeled 'Magnesium Carbonate'). Mid-video includes a stock pain/inflammation image overlay, then capsules shown in the cup, then a printed photo of a patient consultation held up. Final third: holds up Magnesium Complex bottle with trust badges on screen ('3rd party lab tested / Made in USA / Clinical formulation') and closes with '#1 supplement' text overlay while holding bottle close to camera.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Medical authority + unexpected visual (animated anatomical diagram appearing over a health professional) creates two-layer credibility hooks: the scrubs/stethoscope signals expertise, and the instant diagram signals this is educational information you need. The color-change liquid demo creates a visual experiment hook that triggers 'what happens next' from the first pour.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The liquid color-change demo functions as a visual experiment that viewers must watch resolve. Overlaid stock images and printed photos create context-switching that disrupts habituation. The escalating trust-badge reveal at the end (lab tested → Made in USA → clinical formulation → #1 supplement) is a closing sequence that rewards viewers who stayed.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Medical professional endorsing a specific supplement is a trust-transfer mechanism — viewers share with family members and tag partners who have related symptoms. The pain/inflammation overlay and hormone content hit multiple symptom clusters simultaneously, broadening who feels the content is for them. 'My nurse recommended this' is one of the most share-generating frames in supplements.

dimensionRatings.hook: strong — clinician framing + instant anatomical diagram overlay is a scroll-stopper in the health niche; builds curiosity about what the demo will prove
dimensionRatings.format: strong — talking-head with embedded demos and overlays is the highest-converting supplement format; keeps visual interest without losing the authority of the presenter
dimensionRatings.visual: ok — soft-diffused lighting and clean background are professional; the animated diagrams and printed-photo props read slightly low-budget but add authenticity
dimensionRatings.pacing: strong — the demo stages, overlay inserts, and trust-badge finale create natural pacing beats across 99 seconds without relying on cuts alone
